Part B.key_evidence: Single detection (ndethist=1) at a position that has been observed 1966 times, with deltajd=0 and firstmjd=61135.4. The candidate is point-like (classtar=0.98, fwhm=3.28 px, sharpnr=0.022). The nearest PS1 source is 11.95" away (sgscore1=0.99), so it is not a host association. The difference image shows a clear positive residual at center, magpsf=18.58 in r with reasonable SNR.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: Most consistent with a solar-system object (asteroid): a point-like, one-off positive detection at a location previously covered ~2000 times without prior detections, and with no associated host galaxy or coincident PS1 counterpart. A variable star is disfavored because repeated visits produced only one detection. A supernova is disfavored because there is no host galaxy near the candidate.
Part B.alternative_analysis: An alternative is a young supernova or other extragalactic transient caught on first detection; the positive residual and point-like morphology are compatible, but the lack of any underlying host in the reference and nearest PS1 object being a star 12" away make this less likely than an asteroid. A flaring/high-proper-motion stellar source is less consistent with ndethist=1 out of ~2000 coverages.
